ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for deep-fried pork organ dish; closest defensible anchor is pork offal food product (FOODON_03301116). Crispy fried preparation and Vietnamese hawker context not captured in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pig offal — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for fried pork organ dish; no specific code for the prepared/fried form exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers pig offal as a raw commodity. The deep-fried, marinated, crispy preparation is not separately coded. Approximate match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this specific fried pork organ dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of Fried Pork Organ is approximately 150g, representing a single-person portion as served at Singapore food centres.

While the deep-fried coating may contribute minor residual carbohydrate not fully captured in the nutrient data, the total carbohydrate load is negligible and GI testing is not meaningful. GI is set to 0 (numeric sentinel for not-applicable per validator requirement) and gi_category to not_applicable. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 as a result.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050mg per 100g — far exceeding the 600mg/100g upper threshold; potassium at 47mg/100g provides no meaningful counterbalance
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this dish; a single 150g serving delivers approximately 1575mg sodium, representing over 68% of the 2300mg daily limit, with negligible potassium to offset the pressor effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and high saturated fat content drives insulin resistance; high sodium further exacerbates metabolic stress
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish; the deep-fried preparation method produces AGEs that impair insulin signalling, and the very high sodium content (1050mg/100g) contributes to cardiovascular risk already elevated in this population.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050mg/100g), cholesterol (89mg/100g), saturated fat from deep-frying, and AGE formation collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should strictly limit or avoid this dish; the combination of very high sodium, dietary cholesterol, saturated fat from deep-frying, and AGE load presents a compounded cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; pork organs are high in arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifying inflammatory cytokine production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; deep-fried pork organ is among the highest combined sources of dietary AGEs and arachidonic acid, both of which directly amplify joint inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Pork Organ, known in Vietnamese as Thu Linh Chiên Giòn, is a deep-fried pork offal dish served in Singapore's hawker food centres, reflecting the Vietnamese culinary influence present in the local food landscape. The dish typically uses pork organ meats — which may include intestines, liver, or a mixed offal cut — marinated in fish sauce and salt before being coated in a thin layer of cornstarch or rice flour and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The result is a dish with a crunchy exterior and tender, richly flavoured interior, often served with a chilli dipping sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides 171 kcal per 100g, with a high protein content of 19.1g and moderate fat of 10.5g. Carbohydrate is reported as 0.0g by HPB, though the starch coating may contribute minor residual carbohydrate not fully captured in the nutrient data. Sodium is very high at 1050mg per 100g, driven by fish sauce marinade and salt, making this dish a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or Type 2 Diabetes. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are associated with insulin resistance and inflammatory joint conditions. Pork organ meats are also rich in arachidonic acid, an omega-6 fatty acid that can amplify inflammatory cytokine production, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Cholesterol is 89mg/100g. The dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner item in Singapore food centres.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID G00010529, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork offal reference as a supplementary anchor since HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0g by HPB for this entry — confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database, though the deep-fried starch coating may contribute minor residual carbohydrate not fully captured in the nutrient data; the HPB value is accepted as reported. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB — naturally zero for a meat-based dish with no plant fibre components. Saturated_fat_g (3.2g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork offal (NDB 10006) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish; this is an approximate value. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not available in HPB Foodo for this entry, and USDA SR Legacy pork offal data does not provide a sufficiently matched breakdown for the deep-fried preparation. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry, and no sufficiently matched approved-source equivalent was available to provide defensible estimates for the specific deep-fried preparation. Glycemic index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value indicating not-applicable; gi_category is set to not_applicable because this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0g/100g per HPB); GI testing is not meaningful for this food. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0. The dish is not halal due to pork content. Vietnamese name Thu Linh Chiên Giòn is the diacritically correct form; Thu Linh Chien Gion is the romanized/unaccented form used in Singapore hawker signage.
